includedSources.name: Princeton Chen
includedSources.note: 2 emails: Daily News Wall + Weekly Paper Review

includedSources.name: Rupak Ghose
includedSources.note: S&P, Nasdaq and the SpaceX IPO

items.title: Taylor Rule Says Policy Is Already Too Loose: CPI Heading to 4.2%, Real Rates Falling Without a Cut
items.idea: Multiple versions of the Taylor Rule already place the appropriate Fed funds rate ABOVE the current policy level — and that gap widens if CPI accelerates to 4.2% (highest since 2023). Real rates are falling as inflation accelerates, meaning the Fed is effectively easing without touching rates. The paradox: the economy is strong because money has been relatively easy, but the stronger it gets, the harder it becomes to justify keeping money easy.
items.angle: If Taylor Rule models are right, Warsh walks into June 16-17 with models telling him policy is already too loose. The rate-cut consensus that drove markets for two years is not just stale — it may have inverted into a hike debate. Watch for dissent counts in the June FOMC.
items.tweetA: Taylor Rule says the Fed funds rate should be HIGHER than where it sits today. CPI heading to 4.2%. Real rates falling as inflation accelerates. The Fed is effectively easing without a single cut — and the economy is strong enough that this is the last thing it needs.
items.tweetB: The paradox of this cycle: the economy is too strong for easy money, but easy money is why it is strong. Taylor Rule models are no longer pointing toward cuts. They are pointing at 'you are already too loose.' Warsh inherits this June 16-17.

items.title: SpaceX $75B IPO as Stress Test: Can Markets Finance Multiple Gold Rushes Simultaneously?
items.idea: $75B SpaceX IPO (seeking ~$1.8T valuation) hits June 11-12 as cash balances at major brokerages sit near multi-year lows and 600+ ETFs have launched in just 6 months. Anthropic and OpenAI are queuing behind it. The question is not whether demand will show up — it is where that demand comes from, while 30Y yields are above 5% and every speculative trade on the board competes for the same capital.
items.angle: SpaceX IPO is a real-time referendum on risk appetite. If it prices strong with high retail participation, the bull case continues. If it struggles or pulls overallocation from crypto and AI names, it signals the capital pool is hitting its limits just as the AI IPO pipeline is thickest.
items.tweetA: $75B SpaceX IPO hits June 11-12. Cash at brokerages near multi-year lows. 600+ ETFs launched in 6 months. 30Y above 5%. The question is not whether demand will show up. The question is where that demand comes from.
items.tweetB: SpaceX is not a rocket story. It is a capital allocation stress test. Can markets fund the AI revolution, absorb record government debt, support a $75B IPO — AND a wave of AI mega-cap IPOs — all while the 30Y sits above 5%? June 11 is the vote.

items.title: Capital Is Not Infinite: AI, Govts, Crypto, 600+ ETFs, and Mega-IPOs All Competing at Once
items.idea: For two years, every dip got bought because money was always entering the system. Retail participation doubled over 15 years. Meme stocks, crypto, AI — all sustained by an expanding pool. Now: cash balances near multi-year lows, dry powder no longer expanding at the same pace as investor enthusiasm, 600+ ETF launches in 6 months, AI infrastructure buildout demanding trillions, and governments issuing debt at breathtaking pace. Something has to give.
items.angle: The 'buy every dip' regime worked because the pool was growing. The pool is finite. When SpaceX, Anthropic, OpenAI, Treasury issuance, and the AI buildout all compete simultaneously, the cost of capital rises to ration them. That is the macro regime shift of H2 2026.
items.tweetA: Who pays for all of this? AI infrastructure buildout. Record Treasury issuance. 600+ ETF launches in 6 months. $200B+ in AI IPOs queuing. All competing for the same capital pool — while borrowing costs rise and retail cash is near multi-year lows.
items.tweetB: For two years the strategy was: buy every dip. It worked because money was always entering the system. Retail cash at major brokerages is now near multi-year lows. The pool is finite. That math matters now.

items.title: TSMC CEO: AI Chip Supply Will Fall Short for Years, Growth Capped at 30% Near-Term
items.idea: TSMC CEO C.C. Wei bounded AI chip growth at 30% annually for the near term and said global supply will fall short of AI-driven demand for years, even as US fab capacity comes online by end of decade. This holds even with Musk's Terafab initiative (partnering with Intel) trying to build independent fab infrastructure. TSMC also gave employees a 30% bonus bump — demand so robust the talent retention cost is justified. Nvidia and Apple remain TSMC top customers.
items.angle: The constraint on the AI buildout is not models or software — it is fabrication capacity. TSMC setting a 30% growth ceiling while demand is unlimited is the most important supply-side signal in the AI trade. It validates continued semiconductor pricing power for the next several years.
items.tweetA: TSMC CEO bounded AI chip growth at 30% per year for the near term. Supply will fall short of AI demand for YEARS, even as US fab capacity comes online by 2030. Musk's Terafab, Intel partnerships, on-shoring — all of it combined and still not enough. The AI constraint is atoms, not algorithms.
items.tweetB: TSMC handed out 30% employee bonuses this year. Not because margins are easy — because demand they cannot fill requires keeping talent. When the world's most important manufacturer does that, it tells you more about long-term demand than any sell-side note.

items.title: Nvidia Spark DGX: First Windows PCs With Nvidia as Main Processor, Launching Fall 2026
items.idea: At GTC Taipei 2026 (Computex), Jensen Huang announced Spark DGX — Windows computers where Nvidia silicon is the primary processor, not just a GPU add-on. Partners: Dell, HP, Asus, Acer, Microsoft Surface. Designed for local AI agentic computing, bridging Nvidia cloud AI mainframes to offices and homes. Squaring up against Qualcomm, AMD, Intel, and Apple Silicon. Microsoft gets a second chance at AI PC after the Copilot+ stumble.
items.angle: Intel's last moat was being the CPU. Nvidia just stepped into the CPU slot. The OEM list is essentially the entire Windows ecosystem. This is the consumer AI infrastructure play — and it changes the competitive dynamic from the data center down to the desktop.
items.tweetA: Fall 2026: first Windows PCs where Nvidia is the MAIN processor — not a GPU add-on, the primary chip. Dell, HP, Asus, Acer, Microsoft Surface. The OEM list is everyone. Intel just lost its last moat in personal computing.
items.tweetB: Microsoft tried AI PCs with Qualcomm chips. Copilot+ stumbled. Now Nvidia takes the primary chip slot with Spark DGX — designed for local agentic computing. The edge AI infrastructure cycle has arrived, and it runs on Nvidia silicon.

items.title: Anthropic Mythos: Government-Only Enterprise Cybersecurity Model at Premium Prices Pre-IPO
items.idea: Anthropic's next-gen 'Mythos' model is being hand-released to governments and major partners only, at far higher prices than prior models. Enterprise customers are lined up with conviction. Cybersecurity may be the next product-market fit after AI coding — the first vertical where enterprises justify premium pricing at scale. OpenAI's GPT-5.5+ likely to follow a similar premium pricing path. Conveniently timed ahead of Anthropic's confidential IPO filing.
items.angle: The LLM pricing ladder is going vertical. After coding (Claude Code, Cursor, Copilot), cybersecurity is next. Anthropic pricing Mythos at 'mythic' levels pre-IPO is a deliberate demonstration of pricing power for the roadshow.
items.tweetA: Anthropic is releasing its next model hand-delivered to governments and major enterprise partners only — at prices above anything before it. Cybersecurity is the pitch. After coding, it may be the first vertical where AI locks in enterprise and justifies premium pricing at scale.
items.tweetB: The model is called Mythos. It is priced for premium. It is restricted to governments. It arrives right before the IPO roadshow. The name tells you the positioning strategy. The timing tells you the rest.

items.title: AI IPO Flood: Anthropic + OpenAI + SpaceX/xAI Expected to Raise $200B+ in Public Markets
items.idea: Anthropic filed confidentially for IPO ahead of SpaceX/xAI's $1.75T-valuation launch next week. OpenAI has similar plans later in 2026. Together these three are expected to raise over $200 billion from public institutional and individual investors. Alphabet/Google launched an $80B+ equity funding campaign with Berkshire as investor. After years of IPO drought, the argument is that passive fund liquidity (tens of trillions) can absorb it.
items.angle: The sequencing matters. SpaceX first (June 11-12), then Anthropic, then OpenAI. Each successive IPO faces a market that has already been asked to absorb the last one. The pent-up IPO demand thesis holds if rates are stable. It breaks if Warsh signals a hike on June 16-17.
items.tweetA: Anthropic filed for IPO. SpaceX roadshow next week. OpenAI to follow. Three companies expected to raise $200B+ from public markets. Berkshire Hathaway is now a top holder of Alphabet. The institutional AI bet is scaling to public equity at the worst week for tech in a year.
items.tweetB: The IPO drought is ending with a deluge. $200B+ expected from Anthropic, OpenAI, and SpaceX alone. Against a backdrop: BNP forecasting three Fed hikes, 30Y above 5%, crypto in extreme fear, and Nasdaq down 4% on the week. The capital demand is real. The timing is not ideal.

items.title: Berkshire Hathaway Takes $30B+ Stake in Alphabet — Buffett's Second Core Tech Investment After Apple
items.idea: Berkshire Hathaway is now a top-ten holder of Alphabet/Google with a $30B+ stake — joining Apple as only the second core technology investment in Buffett's portfolio. Berkshire is also one of Alphabet's largest investors in their $80B+ equity funding campaign. The investor who famously sat out dotcom, skipped Meta, Amazon, and Netflix for years, is now materially invested in the two dominant AI infrastructure companies dressed in consumer names.
items.angle: Buffett's signal: he is not buying AI hype — he is buying AI infrastructure with durable consumer moats (search, iOS ecosystem). His two core tech holdings (Apple + Google) are both AI infrastructure companies. That framing is worth keeping when evaluating whether the AI trade is a bubble.
items.tweetA: Berkshire Hathaway now holds $30B+ in Alphabet — a top-ten position. That is Buffett's second core tech investment after Apple. Not Microsoft, not Amazon, not Meta. Apple and Google. Both are AI infrastructure companies wearing consumer names.
items.tweetB: Buffett avoided the dotcom. Made one tech exception for Apple. Now made a second for Google at $30B. The man who skipped Facebook and Netflix for a decade decided Google's AI infrastructure is worth more than most of his industrial holdings. One of those signals matters.

items.title: May NFP +172K vs. 85K Consensus — More Than Double; Fed Hike Now Fully Priced by December
items.idea: May nonfarm payrolls printed 172,000 against an 85,000 consensus — more than double. April was revised up to ~179-214K. Unemployment held at 4.3% for a 3rd straight month. Average hourly earnings decelerated to 3.4% YoY (from 3.6%), offering a small wage-relief offset but nowhere near enough. Traders now fully price a quarter-point Fed hike by December (Bloomberg). 10Y yield jumped above 4.5%, 30Y above 5%. Good news is bad news regime fully activated.
items.angle: The wage deceleration (3.4%) is the one dovish data point, but it is overwhelmed by 172K jobs when 85K was expected. Warsh's June 16-17 debut is now a hike-vs-hawkish-hold debate, not a cut discussion. Any hawkish signal from the new chair sends yields higher. The AI buildout financing cost rises directly with the 30Y.
items.tweetA: May payrolls: 172,000. Consensus: 85,000. More than double. April revised up. Wages down to 3.4%. The jobs are there, the inflation is there, the rate cut is not. Traders now fully price a December hike. 10Y above 4.5%, 30Y above 5%. Good news is bad news.
items.tweetB: The consensus was 85,000 jobs. They printed 172,000. That is not a miss — that is a different universe of economic reality. BNP moved from 'stable policy' to 'three hikes' on one payroll report. Warsh's first FOMC is June 16-17. He walks in with this.

items.title: BNP Forecasts Three Fed Hikes; Polymarket 52%; April FOMC Most Dissents Since 1992
items.idea: BNP Paribas abandoned its stable-policy call and now forecasts three Fed hikes starting December. Polymarket shows 52% probability of a rate increase before year-end. The April FOMC vote was 8-4 to hold at 3.50-3.75% — the most dissents since 1992. Warsh is 14 days into the job and his June 16-17 FOMC is immediately a hawkish-hold vs. hike debate, not a cut discussion.
items.angle: 8-4 is not a stable consensus. One more hot number and it could be 7-5. The Fed's internal disagreement is visible and Warsh has not yet established credibility. Markets hate uncertainty about central bank direction — and right now there is maximum uncertainty. Long vol around the June 16-17 FOMC.
items.tweetA: BNP Paribas: three Fed hikes starting December. Polymarket: 52% probability of a rate increase by year-end. April FOMC: 8-4 to hold — most dissents since 1992. This is not a tail risk. The Fed is not unified, the market knows it, and Warsh faces his first test June 16-17.
items.tweetB: April FOMC was 8-4 to hold. Most dissents since 1992. New Fed chair, fragile consensus, hot inflation, hot jobs. The path of least resistance used to be cuts. Now the question is whether to hike. The bond market is not being dramatic.

items.title: Hezbollah Rejects Ceasefire; Iran President Resigned, IRGC Hardliners Ascendant; Hormuz Still Closed
items.idea: Hezbollah rejected Israel's ceasefire offer — re-escalation risk back on. US-Iran negotiations stalled; 60-day MOU framework still unsigned with conflicting statements. Iran President Pezeshkian reportedly resigned, with IRGC hardliners now ascendant. US and Iran traded heavy fire earlier in the week (strikes on Qeshm Island + tankers). Hormuz still effectively closed — it handles ~20% of global oil AND LNG flows. WTI sits at ~$92-95, Brent ~$94-97.
items.angle: Markets priced stalled diplomacy as oil-bearish (deal still possible, Hormuz opens). But IRGC hardliners ascending after the president's resignation changes the political economy of a deal. This is the opposite of what markets priced. Hormuz closed for 90+ days now.
items.tweetA: Hezbollah rejected the ceasefire. Hormuz still closed — that is 20% of global oil AND LNG. Iran president reportedly resigned, IRGC hardliners now ascendant. The market priced stalled diplomacy as bearish for oil. The IRGC has different plans.
items.tweetB: The 60-day MOU is unsigned. US and Iran traded fire on Qeshm Island. The president resigned. IRGC hardliners are in charge. Hormuz closed for 90+ days. And WTI is at $93. The market is pricing this as 'nearly resolved.' Worth revisiting that assumption.

items.title: BTC Below $60K (2026 Low, Fear & Greed 12): Fundstrat Bear Targets Hit, ETF Outflow Streaks Ending
items.idea: BTC hit intraday low of $59,100 — lowest of 2026 — as hot NFP killed rate-cut hopes. Crypto Fear & Greed Index at 12 (extreme fear). Total crypto market cap at ~$2.18T, down ~48% from the $4.2T peak. BUT: three things changed Thursday. Fundstrat H1 2026 bear targets were hit (BTC $60-65K, ETH $1,800-2,000, SOL $50-75). BTC spot ETFs ended their record 13-day/$4.4B outflow streak with a small net positive. ETH ETFs ended a 17-day outflow streak. First tentative demand stabilization.
items.angle: Capitulation hunting, not knife catching. The three-signal convergence (bear targets hit, extreme fear, ETF streaks ending) historically marks exhaustion of the marginal seller. Disciplined entry: scale into high-$50Ks, stop below $56K. Let $63-64K reclaim confirm the low. June 16-17 FOMC is the next binary.
items.tweetA: BTC at $59,100 intraday. Fear and Greed at 12. Crypto down 48% from peak. But three things changed Thursday: Fundstrat bear targets hit, BTC ETF 13-day outflow streak ended, ETH ETF 17-day streak ended. The marginal seller is exhausting. Not done — but close.
items.tweetB: Capitulation hunting checklist: bear price targets hit (BTC $60-65K). Fear and Greed at 12. ETF outflows ended after $4.4B out of BTC funds. Scale into high-$50Ks with a stop below $56K. 'Close to capitulation' and 'time to go all-in' are different things.

items.title: Nasdaq -4.18%, $1T Wiped from Semis: Broadcom Cascade + Defensive Rotation to Colgate, KO, J&J
items.idea: Nasdaq fell 4.18% to 25,709 — biggest drop since 2025 tariff turmoil. S&P 500 -2.64% to 7,383. ~$1 trillion wiped from semiconductors this week. Broadcom's AI guidance miss was the catalyst that cascaded through the chip complex. Defensive rotation visible: Colgate +4%, Coca-Cola +3%, J&J +2% as tech was dumped. S&P snapped a 9-week win streak. AI-buildout names hit hardest as 30Y above 5% raises financing costs.
items.angle: The rotation from AI names to Colgate is the clearest late-cycle signal yet. When investors sell Nvidia to buy Colgate, they are not playing a sector rotation — they are shifting from 'growth on credit' to 'cash generation regardless of rates.' That is the regime change.
items.tweetA: Colgate +4% while Nasdaq -4%. Coca-Cola +3% while semis lost $1 trillion in market cap this week. That is not a sector rotation. That is a regime rotation — from growth-at-any-cost to cash-flow-regardless-of-rates. The late-cycle playbook is running.
items.tweetB: Broadcom's AI guidance miss triggered a $1 trillion drawdown across the semiconductor complex — not just Broadcom, the entire space. One company's forward guide is the fulcrum for the largest investment theme in a generation. That is what concentration risk looks like.

items.title: Samsung 45K-Worker Strike Day 11 of 18: HBM/DRAM Chokepoint at AI Demand Peak
items.idea: Samsung's 45,000-worker strike is in Day 11 of 18 — hitting HBM (High Bandwidth Memory) and DRAM production, which are the physical chokepoints for the entire AI compute buildout. Micron has already confirmed HBM sold out through 2026. The strike hits the second major supplier at the same time semis are being repriced lower on Broadcom's guidance miss. The market is pricing 'AI capex peaking' — the supply chain is showing the opposite constraint.
items.angle: Irony: semis are selling off because Broadcom's AI guide missed, suggesting demand uncertainty. Meanwhile the physical supply of HBM/DRAM is being constrained by a strike at Samsung. Those two signals are in direct contradiction. The one that matters for 2027 AI buildout is probably the supply constraint.
items.tweetA: 45,000 Samsung workers on strike, Day 11 of 18. HBM and DRAM are the chokepoints for the entire AI buildout. Micron says HBM sold out through 2026. Add a strike at the other major supplier into the worst week for chips since 2025. The supply constraint is not easing.
items.tweetB: Semis down $1T on Broadcom's AI guide miss. Same week: Samsung 45K-worker strike at the HBM/DRAM bottleneck. The market is pricing 'AI capex peak.' The supply chain is saying something different.

items.title: Solana DEX Volume -82%: 8th Consecutive Red Monthly Candle — First Ever; Chain Activity Collapsed Before Price
items.idea: SOL at $64, inside Fundstrat's bear target range ($50-75). More important than price: on-chain DEX volume collapsed ~82% as the meme-coin launchpad engine stalled. SOL printed its 8th consecutive red monthly candle — the first such streak in Solana's history. Resistance at $82. The activity that justified the $200+ SOL thesis has evaporated. This is not just price weakness — it is a demand-engine failure.
items.angle: Price targets met but thesis not intact. For a bounce trade, ETH over SOL: ETH's 17-day ETF outflow streak ended, ETH has a cleaner institutional demand story via ETFs, and SOL's DEX volume collapse is a structural signal. A genuine SOL recovery requires on-chain activity recovery first.
items.tweetA: SOL hit the Fundstrat bear target at $64. But the price is not the story. On-chain DEX volume is down 82%. Eight consecutive red monthly candles — the first such streak in Solana's history. When the economic activity that justifies a chain's valuation collapses, a price target is not a floor.
items.tweetB: Solana's meme-coin engine stalled. DEX volume -82%. The thesis for $200 SOL was transaction activity. That activity is gone. Price at target but thesis is not intact. For a bounce trade, ETH is the better vehicle: 17-day ETF outflow streak just ended, and the thesis is structurally intact.

items.title: JPM + BofA + Citi Building Shared Tokenized Network vs. Stablecoins; Fannie Mae Accepts Crypto as Mortgage Collateral
items.idea: JPMorgan, Bank of America, and Citi announced plans to launch a shared tokenized payment network next year — a collective response to the stablecoin threat. Separately: Fannie Mae now accepts crypto as collateral for conventional mortgages; the first Fannie Mae-backed crypto mortgage was issued in Ann Arbor. CFTC cleared Coinbase global crypto perpetual futures via Deribit. House bill aims to ban lawmakers from crypto prediction markets.
items.angle: Two signals pointing in the same direction: traditional financial infrastructure is being rebuilt around crypto assets (Fannie Mae) and against stablecoin competition (JPM+BofA+Citi tokenized network). This is not adoption — it is defensive capitulation from the biggest incumbents.
items.tweetA: Fannie Mae just accepted crypto as collateral for a conventional mortgage. That is $7 trillion of US housing guarantee infrastructure recognizing crypto as a legitimate asset class. The first one closed in Ann Arbor. It will not be the last.
items.tweetB: JPMorgan, BofA, and Citi are building a SHARED tokenized payments network — together — as a response to stablecoins. The three largest US banks just admitted they cannot fight stablecoins individually. The question is not whether crypto wins on payments rails. It is who controls them.

items.title: AI Investment Is Leaking Into the Real Economy: NFP as First Case Study of AI-to-Macro Spillover
items.idea: The AI investment boom is no longer just a data center and earnings story — it is starting to show up in construction, engineering, manufacturing, logistics, and power generation employment. Hundreds of billions deployed to AI infrastructure must eventually appear in economic data. The May NFP report — 172K vs. 85K consensus — may be the first real-world data signal that the AI capex cycle is driving labor demand at macro scale.
items.angle: If AI capex turns into real economy jobs, which turns into stronger NFP, which turns into higher rates, which tightens AI financing conditions — the AI boom contains the seeds of its own constraint. This non-linear feedback loop is what most linear models miss. The strongest argument for the Taylor Rule tightening case.
items.tweetA: The AI investment boom is no longer just a data center story. It is showing up in payroll data. Construction, engineering, logistics, power generation — the sectors that service data center buildouts — are hiring. The largest capex cycle in tech history is entering the real economy.
items.tweetB: NFP at 172K may be the first data point in a multi-year feedback loop: AI capex turns into employment, which turns into consumer spending, which turns into inflation, which forces the Fed to stay tight, which raises AI financing costs. The AI trade and the rate-hike trade may be the same trade.

items.title: Korean Won = Clearest AI Trade Proxy: Technology Has Become a Global Macro Driver for Currencies
items.idea: Korea has become one of the clearest expressions of the global AI trade: semiconductor sentiment, equity flows, and currency performance (KRW) are increasingly intertwined. When the AI buildout accelerates, Korean equities outperform and the KRW rallies. Technology is no longer just an equity market theme — it has evolved into a global macro variable capable of influencing currencies, capital flows, and risk appetite across DM and EM. Old FX frameworks (rate differentials, current account) do not fully capture this.
items.angle: KRW as a real-time AI-sentiment barometer. When the chip cycle turns, the won moves first. Useful for monitoring AI trade momentum without looking at Nvidia's stock. Samsung strike (Day 11) + chip selloff this week should already be showing up in KRW weakness.
items.tweetA: Korea is now the clearest expression of the global AI trade. Semiconductor sentiment moves the KRW. AI buildout momentum moves Korean equities. Tech has evolved from a sector story into a macro variable that drives currencies and capital flows. Old FX frameworks do not capture this.
items.tweetB: Want to know how the AI trade is doing? Watch the Korean won. KRW is the AI proxy currency — semiconductor exports, equity sentiment, and data center demand all feed into it. When the chip cycle turns, the won moves first. Add it to the dashboard.

items.title: S&P Refused SpaceX Fast-Track Into S&P 500 — Index Governance vs. Commercial Pressure, Explained
items.idea: S&P Global Indexes declined to fast-track SpaceX into the S&P 500 despite enormous commercial pressure — a decision that surprised markets. The key structural difference: Nasdaq HAD to say yes (Nasdaq 100 only holds Nasdaq-listed stocks, so refusing SpaceX meant losing the IPO and cascading to OpenAI + Anthropic). S&P could afford to hold its governance standards because S&P 500 is exchange-agnostic — it could add SpaceX later whenever it qualifies. This mirrors how S&P delayed Tesla's inclusion.
items.angle: The question — 'is passive the new active?' — is the right framing. When index inclusion decisions move trillions in passive flows, the index committee IS making active decisions. S&P maintaining standards is the system working. Nasdaq bending them is commercial logic. Both behaviors are rational.
items.tweetA: S&P just said no to fast-tracking SpaceX into the index. Everyone expected them to cave. The key difference: Nasdaq HAD to say yes — Nasdaq 100 only holds Nasdaq-listed stocks. Losing the SpaceX listing meant losing the Nasdaq 100 inclusion permanently. S&P could maintain standards. Nasdaq could not.
items.tweetB: Is passive the new active? When S&P says no and Nasdaq says yes to the same company, trillions in passive flows get directed differently. The index governance decision IS an active portfolio decision. That is what the FT Alphaville question gets right.

charts.desc: 🔥 CHART WORTH SAVING: S&P vs. Nasdaq index revenue comparison + index provider rule comparison chart. Both in the article.

items.title: Nasdaq 100 Rewrote Its Rules for Mega-Cap IPOs: Eliminated Wait Periods and Float Requirements
items.idea: Nasdaq made wholesale changes to Nasdaq 100 rules for mega-cap IPOs: substantially reduced the waiting period post-IPO for index inclusion, abandoned the minimum free-float requirement. CRSP also relaxed (replaced 10% float rule with market-cap criteria). Q1 2026 Nasdaq Index revenues: $220M, up 14% YoY — but still a minnow vs S&P peers. Rupak Ghose draws an explicit 1999 parallel: sell-side analyst adding 'option value' twice in one day to justify a price above where the stock had already run.
items.angle: Nasdaq has ~$220M in index revenues growing 14% YoY but is structurally disadvantaged vs S&P's index franchise. The rule changes are a defensive commercial necessity, not a principled policy choice. The 1999 parallel is pointed: when index methodology becomes a marketing tool for the exchange's listing business, the governance function is impaired.
items.tweetA: Nasdaq Q1 2026 Index revenues: $220M, +14% YoY. Still a minnow vs S&P peers. So they eliminated the wait period for mega-cap IPOs and abandoned the minimum float requirement — for SpaceX. Every rule that existed was designed for a world without SpaceX, Anthropic, and OpenAI.
items.tweetB: Nasdaq 100 only holds Nasdaq-listed stocks. That is not a rule — it is an existential constraint. If SpaceX lists on NYSE, the Nasdaq 100 never gets SpaceX. Both the exchange franchise and the index franchise lose. The rule changes were not optional.

charts.desc: Charts inside — Nasdaq index revenue growth trajectory + index provider comparison. Worth opening.

items.title: SpaceX IPO: Investment Banks and Sell-Side Research Running Compromised by Conflicts of Interest
items.idea: Rupak Ghose (ex-CSFB TMT analyst, 1999 vintage) draws explicit parallels to the dotcom bubble: sell-side equity research now more dependent on investment banking than ever, with shrinking buy-side client bases. Compute deals with Anthropic and Google announced last-minute are possibly short-term IPO support with uncertain long-term margin and value. No one wants to miss SpaceX: wrath-of-trillionaire risk + downstream risk of delaying OpenAI and Anthropic IPOs if SpaceX stumbles.
items.angle: The infrastructure around SpaceX is the tell. When a 1999 veteran TMT analyst explicitly invokes the dotcom parallel on compute deals announced days before a roadshow, that is a practitioner red flag. The question is not the long-term value of SpaceX — it is whether the current ecosystem can price it honestly.
items.tweetA: A 1999 veteran TMT analyst is explicitly invoking the dotcom parallel on SpaceX. Compute deals with Anthropic and Google announced days before the roadshow. Sell-side research more dependent on banking than ever. The ecosystem around this IPO has a conflict-of-interest problem.
items.tweetB: Rupak Ghose was at CSFB in 1999 when a senior analyst added 'option value' twice in one day to justify a dot-com price. He says today is different. He also says some things are the same. Worth reading that framing before June 11.

charts.desc: Index AUM comparison chart in article — shows the relative scale of different index providers.

items.title: HMM + RL Regime Allocation: SPY in Stable Regimes, TLT+GLD in Stressed — Highest Sharpe, Fully Interpretable
items.idea: Fresh paper (5 days old, May 2026): Three-state Gaussian HMM fitted to daily SPY/TLT/GLD data 2004-2025 (low-vol, transitional, high-vol regimes). RL layer learns regime-conditioned allocation with 30% OOS window, one-day execution lag. SPY dominates stable regimes; TLT + GLD protect stressed ones. RL policy achieves highest Sharpe and materially lower drawdowns vs passive SPY. Fully interpretable via discrete regime-to-action mapping.
items.angle: The interpretability claim is the institutional selling point: discrete regime outputs map to concrete actions. Black-box RL cannot get institutional sign-off. Explainable RL on a macro regime framework can. Direct replication template for tactical SPY/TLT/GLD rotation.
items.tweetA: Fresh paper (May 2026): three-state HMM on SPY/TLT/GLD data 2004-2025 plus RL layer. SPY dominates stable regimes. TLT plus GLD protect stressed ones. RL achieves highest Sharpe and lower drawdowns — fully interpretable via discrete regime-to-action mapping. Direct replication template.
items.tweetB: The selling point here is interpretability: discrete regime outputs (low-vol / transitional / high-vol) map to concrete actions. Black-box RL cannot be deployed institutionally. Explainable RL on a macro regime framework can. This bridges that gap cleanly.

charts.desc: Charts inside paper — worth opening for the regime-conditional return distributions.

items.title: Multi-Modal Fed Forecasting: FinBERT on FOMC Text Adds Measurable Alpha Beyond Macro Data Alone
items.idea: 2026 paper: Hybrid models combining structured macro indicators (inflation, employment, GDP) with FinBERT sentiment derived from Fed communications consistently outperform unimodal baselines in predicting fed funds rate shifts. Forward-looking cues in central bank text carry information not present in macro data alone. The uplift is measurable and robust across traditional ML, transformer LMs, and deep learning setups.
items.angle: With Warsh as the new chair (Day 14), the most FOMC dissents since 1992 (8-4), and the market unsure whether to price a hike or hawkish hold, the textual signal from the June 16-17 statement has never been higher-stakes. Reading Warsh's statements through a FinBERT lens is now quantifiably more valuable than waiting for the dot plot.
items.tweetA: You cannot forecast the Fed on macro data alone. A 2026 paper quantifies the gap: hybrid models combining macro indicators with FinBERT sentiment on FOMC communications consistently beat unimodal baselines. The forward-looking information is in the text, not just the data.
items.tweetB: Central bankers telegraph before they act. FinBERT-style sentiment on FOMC statements adds measurable alpha beyond inflation, employment, and GDP. With Warsh 14 days in and 8-4 FOMC dissents, what he says on June 16-17 carries more signal than any macro print this month.

items.title: AMM Liquidity Pools as Mean Field Games: First Formal Framework for Optimal DEX Execution
items.idea: New paper (v3, 2026): Models Uniswap-style AMM liquidity pools as a mean-field game. Links individual trader actions to changes in pool reserves through the AMM invariant curve. Key finding: aggregate trader behavior moves execution price through the invariant curve — you are not moving against a passive pool, you are in a Nash equilibrium with every other trader's flow. First serious MFG treatment of AMMs.
items.angle: Any DEX execution strategy that treats the pool as passive is systematically wrong. The MFG framework shows the correct model: your own flow moves price as an equilibrium outcome, not as an exogenous impact. Relevant for anyone doing optimal execution or LP strategy on DEXs.
items.tweetA: The first rigorous mean-field game treatment of AMM liquidity pools: your trade execution on a DEX is an equilibrium where aggregate trader behavior moves price through the invariant curve. Optimal DEX execution now has a formal framework. DeFi quant has grown up.
items.tweetB: The permanent impact term in Almgren-Chriss is really aggregate market-maker quoting behavior. The execution price on Uniswap is a mean-field equilibrium. Two different papers, same insight: the exogenous impact you assumed is actually endogenous. The models need to catch up.

charts.desc: Mathematical framework — worth reading for DEX execution strategy.

items.title: CTA Replication via Bayesian Decomposition: Long-Horizon Sleeve Is the Sharpe Anchor; Short-Term Adds Convexity
items.idea: Bayesian graphical decomposition of CTA returns across equity/bond/FX/commodity futures: horizon-specific lookback-straddle factors (20-500 day windows). Key finding: the lookback-straddle delta acts as a drift filter; the Sharpe-optimal blend tilts heavily toward the long-horizon component. Short-horizon trend adds convexity and diversification — not core alpha. Disentangles when each horizon adds value.
items.angle: For anyone allocating to CTAs or trend funds: if your manager has both short and long horizon components and you allocate equally between them, you are over-paying for the short-horizon sleeve relative to its Sharpe contribution. The Bayesian decomposition gives you a live attribution tool.
items.tweetA: CTA replication decomposed via Bayesian graphical model across equity, bond, FX, and commodity futures: the long-horizon sleeve is the Sharpe anchor. The short-horizon component adds convexity and diversification — not alpha. Know which sleeve you are paying for.
items.tweetB: If your CTA allocation has both short and long horizon components and you weight them equally: the long-horizon sleeve is doing the work. Short-horizon is convexity and diversification, not the core return generator. The Sharpe-optimal blend tilts long. Audit your blend.
